steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Command of Evidence (Quantitative) question. The team concluded that spray coating "holds promise for improving the power conversion efficiency" over spin coating. To support that conclusion, the data must demonstrate that spray-coated ETLs outperform spin-coated ETLs. The most decisive evidence would show spray coating's worst result beats spin coating's best result.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Both the ETL applied through spin coating and the ETL applied through spray coating showed a power conversion efficiency greater than 10% at their lowest performing thickness.': True, but showing both methods exceed 10% does not establish any advantage of spray over spin. It gives no comparative basis for the conclusion. ✗
  • Choice B — 'The lowest performing ETL applied through spray coating had a higher power conversion efficiency than the highest performing ETL applied through spin coating.': This shows that even spray coating's worst result (~14–16%) beats spin coating's best result (~12–13%). That definitively proves spray coating outperforms spin coating across the board, directly supporting the conclusion. ✓
  • Choice C — 'The highest performing ETL applied through spray coating showed a power conversion efficiency of approximately 13%, while the highest performing ETL applied through spin coating showed a power conversion efficiency of approximately 11%.': The values cited are incorrect per the graph (spray coating's highest is above 13%, spin coating's highest is between 12% and 14%). Incorrect data cannot support the conclusion. ✗
  • Choice D — 'There was a substantial difference in power conversion efficiency between the lowest and highest performing ETLs applied through spray coating.': This describes variation within spray coating alone, not a comparison between spray and spin coating. It does not address whether spray coating is superior to spin coating. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
